coredata.dc:title: BiVO4 nanocrystals with controllable oxygen vacancies induced by Zn-doping coupled with graphene quantum dots for enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Qingguang
Yang, Kunran
Wang, Guoliang
Li, Dongdong
Sun, Jing
Yang, Bo
Zou, Zhiqing
Hu, Weibo
Wen, Ke
Yang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: The weak electron-hole pair separation and transfer of the BiVO4 photoanode restrain its photoelectrochemical performance of water splitting. In this work, we focus on Zn doping to replace Bi-sites within BiVO4 nanocrystals to promote efficient charge separation and transfer. Theoretical and experimental results show that Zn doping induces oxygen vacancies with controllable content. Zn doping and oxygen vacancies not only shift the conduction and valance band positions of BiVO4, resulting a local built-in electric field, but also increase the carrier density, which would be beneficial for charge separation and transfer. In the meantime, water adsorption on Bi-sites is also activated, which would help water splitting. As a result, these contributions synergistically enhance photoelectrochemical performance with the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 34% at 0.6 V vs. RHE, which is much higher than that of pristine BiVO4. Furthermore, by sequentially electrodepositing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cobalt phosphate (Co-Pi) nano-film, we have constructed a hybrid Zn-BiVO4/GQDs/Co-Pi structure to broaden the light absorption and to enhance the stability, its IPCE reaches as high as 57% and photocurrent density achieves 3.01 mA cm−2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, which is 8.6 times of the pristine BiVO4, thus providing an efficient strategy for the structure design of BiVO4 based photoelectrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zn dopant
BiVO4
Oxygen vacancies
GQDs
Water splitting

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of BiVO4 with different Zn-dopant contents. Vertical lines indicate the JCPDS diffraction peaks of monoclinic scheelite BiVO4 (pink, JCPDS #14-0688) and FTO (cyan); (b) Raman spectra of BiVO4 and 3Zn-BiVO4; (c) XPS spectra of O 1 s peaks of BiVO4 and 3Zn-BiVO4; (d) Oxygen vacancies EPR signals of BiVO4 with different Zn-dopant contents.

PEC performance of the BiVO4, 1Zn-BiVO4, 3Zn-BiVO4 and 5Zn-BiVO4 electrodes in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH = 7). (a) LSV curves under AM 1.5G (100 mW cm−2) illumination; (b) charge separation efficiency (
η
sep
); (c) Mott-Schottky plots; (d) IPCE spectra collected at an incident wavelength range from 380 to 600 nm at 0.6 V vs RHE.

(a) Optimized structure of pristine BiVO4(0 1 0) and Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) where Zn substitutes a Bi atom and an oxygen vacancy exists at O2-site (see Fig. S4c and d), (b) Total DOS of pristine BiVO4(0 1 0) surface and Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) surfaces.

The adsorption structure and corresponding charge density difference of H2O adsorption on pristine BiVO4(0 1 0) and Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) surfaces. The charge density difference figures are drawn by VESTA [50]. Yellow parts mean charge gain and the cyan ones indicate charge loss. The isosurfaces are identical.

Electron microscopic characterization of the ZBGC electrode. (a) SEM top-view image; (b) TEM image; (c) the corresponding HRTEM image; (d) SEM side-view image; (e) SEM-EDX elemental mapping images for Zn, Bi, V, O, C, and Co; (f) PL emission spectra of GQDs at the excitation wavelength from 380 to 500 nm, the inset is TEM image of GQDs; (g) UV–Vis spectra of the BiVO4, Zn-BiVO4 and ZBG electrodes.

PEC performance of the BiVO4, Zn-BiVO4, ZBG, ZBGC electrodes. LSV curves under AM 1.5G (100 mW cm−2) illumination in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH = 7); (b) Surface-charge-transfer efficiency (
η
trans
); (c) ABPE curves; (d) IPCE spectra at 0.6 V vs RHE in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH = 7); (e) J-t curves at 1.23 V vs RHE under AM 1.5G illumination. (f) Detection of H2 and O2 evolution at 1.23 V vs RHE.
Schematic of the proposed band gap for the ZBGC electrode.

head.ce:title: BiVO4 nanocrystals with controllable oxygen vacancies induced by Zn-doping coupled with graphene quantum dots for enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201210, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201210, China (H. Yang).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The weak electron-hole pair separation and transfer of the BiVO4 photoanode restrain its photoelectrochemical performance of water splitting. In this work, we focus on Zn doping to replace Bi-sites within BiVO4 nanocrystals to promote efficient charge separation and transfer. Theoretical and experimental results show that Zn doping induces oxygen vacancies with controllable content. Zn doping and oxygen vacancies not only shift the conduction and valance band positions of BiVO4, resulting a local built-in electric field, but also increase the carrier density, which would be beneficial for charge separation and transfer. In the meantime, water adsorption on Bi-sites is also activated, which would help water splitting. As a result, these contributions synergistically enhance photoelectrochemical performance with the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 34% at 0.6 V vs. RHE, which is much higher than that of pristine BiVO4. Furthermore, by sequentially electrodepositing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cobalt phosphate (Co-Pi) nano-film, we have constructed a hybrid Zn-BiVO4/GQDs/Co-Pi structure to broaden the light absorption and to enhance the stability, its IPCE reaches as high as 57% and photocurrent density achieves 3.01 mA cm−2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, which is 8.6 times of the pristine BiVO4, thus providing an efficient strategy for the structure design of BiVO4 based photoelectrodes.

ce:section.ce:para: To overcome above barriers, extensive efforts have been made to boost the charge separation and transfer of BiVO4, including heterojunction engineering [7–9], metal deposition [10,11], and doping [12–15]. Among these strategies, the doping not only regulates electronic structure of BiVO4 and introduces a local built-in electric field to facilitate charge separation, but also induces controllable defects as active sites to accelerate the charge transfer by increasing carrier density [12–14,16]. A typical example is that the V-sites of BiVO4 are substituted by Mo or W doping, leading to an increased charge donor density and a distributed n+-n homojunction, therefore, promoting charge separation and transfer for effective photocatalysis [12,14,17]. The substitution of O-sites within BiVO4 with Se or N induces oxygen vacancies as electron donors, thus enhancing major carrier density and improving carrier mobility [13,16]. As reported, the replacement of Bi-sites within BiVO4 by Sr and Mo doping has also created surface oxygen vacancies, which facilitates the carrier migration or strengthens water adsorption [17–19]. Besides, surface Zn doping by substituting Bi-sites of BiVO4 lowers the Fermi level of the outer doped layer to enhance PEC performance [20]. However, it is still a big challenge to identify the active sites and to deduce the enhanced mechanism of element doped BiVO4 for water splitting. Especially, the roles of doped sites on crystal defects and the doped sites for water adsorption need to be further excavated. Further, could dopants be doped into the bulk BiVO4 and the content of dopants be optimized by a convenient method to significantly improve the PEC performance?

In this work, we focus on Zn dopant with controllable concentration by replacing Bi-sites of BiVO4 nanocrystals via the convenient electrodeposition and calcination method to promote efficient charge separation and transfer. It is found that the Zn doping not only generates tunable oxygen vacancies as electron donors, resulting in the incremental carrier density, but also presents an influence on energy bands of BiVO4 to accelerate charge separation and interfacial charge transfer with the aid of oxygen vacancies. In the meantime, the surface Zn-sites are energetic, the same as Bi-sites to adsorb water molecules, further Zn doping and oxygen vacancies simultaneously activate water adsorption on Bi-sites and are conductive to water splitting. All these contributions lead to a greatly enhanced PEC performance of water splitting. To further improve the light absorption and electrode stability, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cobalt phosphate (Co-Pi) nano-film have been incorporated to construct a hybrid Zn-BiVO4/GQDs/Co-Pi (ZBGC) electrode, which exhibits an outstanding PEC performance.

The pristine and Zn-doped BiVO4 electrodes were prepared by improved electrodeposition and calcination processes, as reported by Kim and Choi [5]. GQDs were fabricated from graphene powder by a modified Staudenmaier method [21,22]. The detailed preparation process of BiVO4/Zn-BiVO4 and GQDs are elaborated in the Supporting Information. According to the molar percent ratio of zinc acetylacetonate to vanadyl acetylacetonate (1/99, 3/97 and 5/95) used during the synthesis, we denoted the Zn-doped BiVO4 electrodes as 1Zn-BiVO4, 3Zn-BiVO4, and 5Zn-BiVO4, respectively.

Zn-BiVO4/GQDs (ZBG) electrodes: Firstly, the pH value of GQDs solution was adjusted to 9 with 0.01 M NaOH to make Z-potential value of GQDs solution below zero. Then, GQDs-decorated Zn-BiVO4 electrodes were prepared using an electrophoretic deposition approach. Anodic deposition was performed potentiostatically at 2 V vs. a FTO counter electrode with a two-electrode system at room temperature for 20, 40 and 60 min to deposit GQDs, the electrodes, denoted as ZBG20, ZBG40 and ZBG60, were then rinsed by DI water and dried under atmospheric environment.

Zn-BiVO4/GQDs/Co-Pi (ZBGC) electrodes: Deposition of Co-Pi was performed by a modified electrodeposition process [23,24]. The BiVO4-based electrodes were immersed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7) with 0.5 mM Co(NO3)2·6H2O. Anodic deposition was implemented at 0.9 V vs. Ag/AgCl at room temperature for 500 s with a three-electrode system, where the BiVO4–based electrodes were used as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (4 M KCl) and platinum wire were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. After the catalyst was deposited, the electrode, denoted as ZBGC, was rinsed with DI water to remove any excess cobalt ions.

The crystallinity and purity of as-deposition electrodes were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu Kα radiation: λ = 1.5418 Å). The morphology of electrodes was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, EDX detector on the Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F30). The valence states of the elements were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Mg Kα radiation: 1253.6 eV). All XPS spectra were calibrated by C 1 s (284.8 eV). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a spectrofluorophotometer (PLS920). UV-vis spectra were recorded using a UV-vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent, Cary 5000) via an integrating sphere. Electronic paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements were performed by an EPR spectrometer (Bruker, EMXplus).
Density functional theory (DFT) simulations and some computational formulas were elaborated in the Supporting Information.

As shown in Fig. S1 (in the Supporting Information), all the PEC performance measurements were conducted with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, CH Instruments Inc.) in a three-electrode system via an H-type electrolytic cell with Nafion 115 as separator using the BiVO4-based electrode as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (4 M KCl) electrode as the reference electrode, and the platinum wire as the counter electrode. The illumination source was under simulated AM 1.5G (100 mW cm−2) solar illumination with a 500 W xenon lamp. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was characterized from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz under illumination. Mott-Schottky values were measured via a frequency of 1000 Hz under the dark. Incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was measured on a monochromator amplifier assembly (Zolix Solar Cell Scan 100) in a three-electrode cell at 0.6 V vs. RHE by an Xe arc lamp. H2 and O2 evolutions were tested by an online gas chromatograph (Shanghai Fanwei Co., Ltd, GC9860), connected with H-type electrolytic cell.

XRD patterns in Fig. 1 a shows that the as-deposited Zn-doped samples are the monoclinic scheelite structure (JCPDS #14–0688), exception for the diffraction peaks for SnO2 from FTO substrate. The highly crystallized samples manifest that Zn doping has no obvious effect on the original crystalline form of BiVO4. In Fig. S2, the magnified analysis of XRD patterns shows that Bragg peaks of BiVO4 shift to higher 2θ values after Zn-doping, which is consistent with the unit cell shrinkage according to the Bragg equation (2dsinθ = n λ ) [25,26]. If Zn2+ (0.083 nm) replaces V5+ (0.035 nm) or exists in the interstitial sites, the decrease in cell parameters cannot be observed, thus confirming a substitutional replacement of larger Bi3+ (0.111 nm) with smaller Zn2+ (0.083 nm) [20,25]. To investigate the possible effect of Zn doping on the electronic structure of BiVO4, XPS spectra of BiVO4 and 3Zn-BiVO4 are shown in Fig. S3. The asymmetric peaks of Bi (4f5/2, 4f7/2) and V (2p1/2, 2p3/2) for Zn-BiVO4 shift to higher binding energies than those for the pristine BiVO4, respectively, which might originate from the interactions between Bi, V atoms and doping Zn atoms [27]. The concentrations of Zn dopant within all the samples could be obtained (Table S1, in the Supporting Information) and the measured atomic content of Zn2+ in 3Zn-BiVO4 is 0.44%. Whereas, the atomic content of Bi in Zn-BiVO4 is less than that in the pristine BiVO4, but for V in reverse, suggesting that Zn2+ would favorably substitute Bi3+ in BiVO4. Raman spectra in Fig. 1b present nearly no shift of the intense band at 825, 367 and 327 cm−1 related to the symmetric stretching mode, symmetric and asymmetric bending modes of V–O in VO4 3- units, respectively [28,29], assessing that Zn doping does not change the lattice of VO4 3- and that Zn2+ might occupy the Bi3+ sites rather than V5+ sites in BiVO4 [30]. To further assess the doping position of Zn2+ within BiVO4, we have conducted DFT calculation. As shown in Fig. S4, the simulated formation energy of Zn2+ substitution of the Bi-site is 2.06 eV, which is lower than that Zn2+ substitution of the V-site (3.67 eV) [31–34], again demonstrating that Zn2+ would prefer to substitute Bi3+ sites rather than V5+ sites within BiVO4.

To explore the possible effect of Zn dopant on the formation of oxygen vacancies, Fig. 1c and S3a depict O 1 s XPS spectra of the samples. The fitted O 1 s peaks at 529.9, 530.2, and 532.1 eV correspond to the lattice oxygen atoms (OL), hydroxyl groups bonded to the metal cations in oxygen vacancy region (OV), and chemisorbed or dissociated oxygen species from the water molecules (OC), respectively [35–38]. From the change in peak area of OV in Fig. S5a and Table S2, one can conclude that the content of oxygen vacancies increase with the concentration of Zn doping from BiVO4 to 5Zn-BiVO4. In fact, from EPR signals of Zn-BiVO4 with an increased Zn dopant in Fig. 1d, an enhanced intensity is observed in comparison with that for pristine BiVO4 with g = 2.004, assigned as oxygen vacancies [37,39], consistent with the XPS result. As reported, PL could be induced by surface oxygen vacancies [4,40]. As observed in Fig. S5b, PL peaks of Zn-BiVO4 are obviously more intense than that of the pristine BiVO4, indicative of an increase in the content of surface oxygen vacancy or surface defect owing to the appropriate amount of Zn dopant, consistent with EPR results, probably accompanying with the enhanced photocatalytic activities [4,40]. Clearly, Zn dopant induces controllable amount of oxygen vacancies, and oxygen vacancies with appropriate concentration would exhibit an enhanced photo-excitation activity.

PEC performance on the BiVO4-based electrodes was evaluated in a typical three-electrode system in 0.1 M phosphate buffer with and without 1 M Na2SO3 as a hole scavenger under AM 1.5G illumination. Fig. 2 a displays that the 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode exhibits the best PEC performance among all the investigated samples. The photocurrent density of 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode (3.06 mA cm−2) is higher than that of pristine BiVO4 electrode (1.66 mA cm−2) at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is much higher than that of previously reported surface Zn doping BiVO4 (∼0.6 mA cm−2) [20]. However, photocurrent density of the 5Zn-BiVO4 electrode is lower than that of 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode, which could be explained that excess oxygen vacancies by Zn doping might become the recombination center of electron-hole pairs [12], in good agreement with above PL experimental result. To reduce the influence of charge transfer to electrolyte interfaces, the electrodes were further characterized in 0.1 M phosphate buffer with 1 M Na2SO3 as a hole scavenger because the charge transfer rate is very quick and surface recombination of carriers could be considered negligibly in sulfite oxidation. Similarly, the largest photocurrent density is observed for the 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode (3.40 mA cm−2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE), as shown in Fig. S6. To understand the effect of Zn doping on separation of electron-hole pairs,
η
sep
were calculated and analyzed [4,27,28,41] in Fig. 2b referring to the light harvest efficiency (
η
Abs
) and photocurrent assuming 100% absorption photon-to-current efficiency (
J
abs
) in Fig. S8c. The 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode manifests better
η
sep
than the pristine BiVO4 electrode in Fig. 2c and
η
sep
of 5Zn-BiVO4 electrode is lower than that of 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode, confirming that the overmuch oxygen vacancies within the 5Zn-BiVO4 electrode decrease the charge separation, in agreement with the PL result. To explore the variation of energy band and carrier densities, Mott-Schottky measurements indicate that the flat band potentials of Zn-doped BiVO4 electrodes are lower than that of the BiVO4 electrodes, which is beneficial for the electron accumulation and transport with increased carrier densities [2,42], as shown in Fig. 2c and Table S3. Moreover, Fig. S7 displays the valance band XPS spectra of BiVO4 and 3ZnBiVO4. The pristine BiVO4 shows a VB maximum (VBM) at 2.28 eV, whereas 3Zn-BiVO4 has an uplifted (left shift) VBM at 1.88 eV. From IPCE measurements at 0.6 V vs. RHE in 0.1 M phosphate buffer in Fig. 2d, the photo-active range of the electrodes covered between 380 and 505 nm in visible light region and the 3Zn-BiVO4 electrode achieves an IPCE of 34% at 400 nm, which is higher than that for the pristine BiVO4 (8%) and for the 5Zn-BiVO4 (29%). Obviously, oxygen vacancies with proper concentration induced by Zn doping could enhance
η
sep
and final photon-to-charge conversion efficiencies.

Considering that Zn substitution and oxygen vacancies are present simultaneously within the Zn-doped BiVO4, we further investigated the electronic structure and chemical reactivity of the pristine and Zn-doped BiVO4 with oxygen vacancies by systematic DFT calculations [43–45]. Since the (0 1 0) facet is the most active surface of BiVO4 for water splitting reaction [18,44,46], we choose the (0 1 0) facet as model. At first, we examined the formation energies of oxygen vacancy on the pristine and Zn-doped BiVO4(0 1 0) surface as shown in Fig. S4b, c. It was found that oxygen vacancy at O2-site has a formation energy of 0.33 eV, much smaller than that at O1-site of pristine BiVO4(0 1 0) (3.26 eV) or other oxygen sites. Therefore, the structure of Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0), i.e. Zn-doped BiVO4 with oxygen vacancy at O2-site, can be feasibly formed.

Fig. 3 b compares the density of state (DOS) results between pristine BiVO4(0 1 0) and Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) to analyze the difference of electronic structure. Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) has a smaller bandgap than that pristine BiVO4(0 1 0), however, the UV-vis spectra in Fig. 5g suggest similar bandgaps for BiVO4 and Zn-BiVO4 [47]. The discrepancy between experimental and theoretical results may result from the different Zn-dopant concentrations that the modeled structure possesses higher Zn concentration (6.25%) than the experiments (0.44%). Meanwhile, the valance band maximum (VBM), conduction band minimum (CBM) and Fermi level upshift obviously after Zn doping partially substitutes for Bi-sites in Table S4, which shows the similar trend to the valence band XPS spectra in Fig. S7. Therefore, a local built-in electric field in the homojunction between pristine and Zn-doped BiVO4 could be constructed, effectively boosting the charge separation efficiency [27,48]. It should also be noticed that impurity levels are near conduction band, which serve as electron donor states, and hence increasing carrier densities to facilitate the charge transfer[13,25,49], in agreement with the Mott-Schottky results. This could explain the enhanced photocurrent density caused by Zn doping, as shown in Fig. 2.

We further evaluated the adsorption energies of H2O (
E
ads
) on pristine BiVO4(0 1 0) and Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) surfaces, as shown in Fig. 4
, since this is the first step during the water-splitting reaction. Bi sites bind H2O most strongly among all surface sites (referring to structure in Fig. S4 and data in Table S5), which is consistent with previous results that H2O tends to adsorb on Bi-sites owing to the saturated coordination of the VO4 tetrahedron [16–18]. The doped Zn-site adsorbs H2O with
E
ads
of −0.48 eV, the same as
E
ads
of Bi-site of pristine BiVO4(0 1 0). Importantly, an oxygen vacancy is near Bi2-site of Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) surface (in Fig. S4d) and H2O adsorbs at the Bi2-site with
E
ads
of −0.60 eV, stronger than that at Bi1-site of pristine BiVO4(0 1 0). These results show that Zn-dopant and oxygen vacancy would activate nearby Bi-sites and lead to the enhanced H2O adsorption. In addition, the charge density difference analysis shows higher charge transfer between H2O and the surface on Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) than pristine BiVO4(0 1 0), consistent with the trend of
E
ads
of H2O. The strong adsorption H2O and charge transfer would facilitate O2 evolution reaction [16,17,51]. The theoretical simulations expound that Zn-BiVO4-Ov(0 1 0) exhibits high activity benefiting from the activation of surface Bi atoms by Zn replacement and oxygen vacancies, reproducing the experimental results.

To further enhance the light absorption and electrode stability, GQDs and Co-Pi nano-film have been introduced to construct a hybrid ZBGC electrode. As SEM and TEM images shown in Fig. 5a, d and S6, the columnar-like Zn-BiVO4 nanocrystals with a mean thickness of ca. 1 μm were grown on FTO, similar to that of the pristine BiVO4 (in Fig. S9). Then, GQDs with a diameter of 5–6 nm (Fig. 5f) were randomly distributed on Zn-BiVO4 (Fig. 5a, S6) to broaden the light response range of the electrodes since the emission wavelength of GQDs (peak at ∼525 nm) matches with the absorption edge of Zn-BiVO4 (∼505 nm) in Fig. 5f and g. After the introduction of GQDs, the ZBG electrode demonstrates an enhanced absorption comparing with the BiVO4 and Zn-BiVO4 electrodes, especially in the wavelength range above 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 5g. Next, the deposited Co-Pi layer with a thickness of less than 10 nm, as an effective co-catalyst to strengthen the electrode stability for water oxidation [23], presents an amorphous structure (on the surface of ZBG, Fig. 5b). HRTEM image of the ZBGC electrode in Fig. 5c displays that the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (0 5 1) plane of BiVO4 (JCPDS No.14–0688) and the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm is related to the (0 0 2) plane of graphitic carbon. The homogeneous element signals of the ZBGC electrode, including Zn, Bi, V, O, C, Co, can be observed in Fig. 5e, indicative of a uniform element distribution. No obvious diffraction peaks are seen for GQDs in Fig. S10, probably due to small percentage and high dispersion of GQDs.

PEC performance on the BiVO4-based electrodes were performed and shown in Fig. 6 to investigate the contributions of GQDs and Co-Pi to water splitting. In the following discussion, the Zn-BiVO4 refers to the aforementioned 3Zn-BiVO4 and the optimal electrophoresis time of GQDs on electrodes is 40 min according to Fig. S11. Fig. 6a shows that the ZBG electrode exhibits greater photocurrent densities than Zn-BiVO4 electrode. After loading Co-Pi, the photocurrent density of the ZBGC electrode reaches 5.03 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is larger than that of the ZBG electrode (4.58 mA cm−2). Notably, the photocurrent density of the ZBGC electrode (3.01 mA cm−2) is 8.6 times of the pristine BiVO4 electrode (0.35 mA cm−2) at 0.6 V vs. RHE. Similarly, the ZBGC electrode achieves the best performance among all the electrodes in the presence of hole scavengers, as shown in Figs. S12 and S13. The surface-charge-transfer efficiencies (
η
trans
) of the different electrodes were calculated from LSV curves and shown in Fig. 6b. The enhanced
η
tran
for the Zn-BiVO4 electrode benefits from the energetic H2O adsorption on Zn-BiVO4 activated by Zn-dopant and oxygen vacancies, as mentioned above. According to the Fig. 6b, GQDs and Co-Pi can further improve the charge transfer. Fig. 6c shows that the maximum ABPE of 1.82% at 0.59 V vs. RHE for the ZBGC electrode is superior to that of the ZBG (1.65% at 0.61 V vs. RHE), Zn-BiVO4 (1.11% at 0.61 V vs. RHE) and pristine BiVO4 (0.29% at 0.86 V vs. RHE) electrodes. The similar trend is observed in Fig. S14 in the presence of hole scavengers. These results reveal that the Co-Pi and CQDs enable the maximum ABPE of the ZBGC electrode at a lower bias than Zn-BiVO4 electrode, which is beneficial for constructing PEC cells [5]. Evidently, the IPCE and APCE are improved after the incorporation of GQDs and Co-Pi, as shown in Fig. 6d and S15, S16. The ZBG and ZBGC electrodes cover broad band photo-active range from 380 to 600 nm and display remarkably enhanced values with IPCEs of 53% and 57% at 400 nm, respectively, definitely assessing that GQDs can increase the light harvest efficiency (Fig. S8).

Furthermore, the electrical performance for charge transport and transfer can be characterized by fitting the Nyquist plots of different electrodes from the EIS measurements under illumination [52,53] (in Figs. S17 and Table S6). EIS spectra reveal that the series resistances (RS) are similar for the different electrodes. The Zn-BiVO4 electrode shows a lower charge transfer resistance in the bulk (RCT1) than the pristine BiVO4 electrode, owing to the increase of the free carrier density after Zn-doping. Moreover, the interfacial charge transfer resistance (RCT2) of Zn-BiVO4 electrode is also smaller than that of the pristine BiVO4 electrode, indicating that Zn doping can facilitate the charge transfer in the electrode/electrolyte interface [52,53]. After the introduction of GQDs and Co-Pi, RCT1 and RCT2 of the Zn-BiVO4 electrode further decrease, again reflecting that GQDs and Co-Pi further improve charge transfer both in the bulk electrodes and the electrode/electrolyte interface as aforementioned. Fig. 6e displays the operational stability of the different electrodes. The ZBGC electrode shows stable photocurrent response than the others, although a slight decay of around 5% is observed after 10 h under consecutive light illumination profiting from Co-Pi decoration. Furthermore, H2 and O2 evolutions were measured by an online gas chromatograph (Fig. 6b and Fig. S18) and the total amounts of H2 and O2 are 882 and 440 μmol, respectively, after a 10 h test, close to a theoretical molar ratio of 2:1 with a Faradaic efficiency of ca. 95%. The amount of produced H2/O2 on the ZBGC electrode is 8-fold more than that on the pristine BiVO4 electrode, definitely demonstrating that the ZBGC electrode exhibits a prominent PEC performance. We have made a comparison with the other reported BiVO4 based catalysts in Table S7. The ZBGC electrode obtains the outstanding PEC performance and overmatches the most reported BiVO4 based catalysts, although, it still cannot reach the state-of-the-art reports.

Above synergetic enhancement effects of the structure engineering could be deduced, as shown in Fig. 7 . When the simulated sunlight radiates on the ZBGC electrode, the photo-generated electrons and holes could be excited simultaneously from the Zn-BiVO4 and GQDs. After Zn doping, oxygen vacancies could be generated, which will cause the increased carrier density and the upshift of valance and conduction bands of BiVO4. From a local perspective, a built-in electric field in the homojunction between doped and pristine crystals could be formed, thus effectively boosting the charge separation and interfacial charge transfer. GQDs as the photosensitizer broaden the light absorption of the Zn-BiVO4 electrode. Moreover, electrons and holes rapidly transfer with the aid of GQDs, which serve as charge transfer mediator. Co-Pi could trap holes from Zn-BiVO4 and GQDs [54]. Then holes oxidize Co2+ to Co3+ and Co4+. Next, Co4+ reduces to Co2+ and oxidizes OH– to O2. Following, repeat the circulation of Co2+, Co3+ and Co4+. Benefiting from the self-circulation of Co-Pi, the ZBGC electrode could exhibit excellent stability for water splitting.

In summary, we have proposed an efficient strategy for Zn doping to replace Bi-sites within the bulk BiVO4 to enhance the PEC performance of water splitting. The Zn doping produced oxygen vacancies with variable concentrations acted as electron donors, leading to an increased carrier density and to a shift in energy band positions of BiVO4, which is beneficial for the charge separation and transfer due to a local built-in electric field. DFT calculations assess that Zn-sites show similar activation to Bi-sites for H2O adsorption, furthermore, it is found that the Zn doping and oxygen vacancies jointly stimulate Bi-sites to adsorb H2O and bring the reinforced H2O adsorption, which would be favorable for water splitting. Therefore, these contributions synergistically promote PEC performance for the Zn-BiVO4 electrode with an IPCE of 34% at 400 nm and the great photocurrent density of 1.85 mA cm−2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, much higher than the reported results. By incorporating GQDs and Co-Pi onto the Zn-BiVO4 electrode, a hybrid ZBGC electrode is constructed. The introduction of GQDs extends the light absorption and facilitates the charge transfer. The incorporation of Co-Pi film reinforces the electrode stability. As a result, the ZBGC electrode exhibits an excellent PEC performance with a prominent IPCE of 57% at 400 nm and a remarkable photocurrent density of 3.01 mA cm−2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE. The total amounts of H2 and O2 are 882 and 440 μmol on the ZBGC electrode with a Faradaic efficiency of ca. 95%, respectively, after a 10 h test, which are 8 times higher than those on the pristine BiVO4 electrode. The above results indicate that the structure design is a valuable strategy to construct efficient photoelectrodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0206500, 2018YFB1502400), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21533005, 21673275), Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (19YF1452900). We thank the HPC Platform of ShanghaiTech University for computing time.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.04.161.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
